name: How We Use Mathematics
description:
Mathematics is not just for mathematicians. It is used in diverse ways in society, and by scientists of every hue. Even if they don't prove theorems, non-mathematicians sometimes use fairly sophisticated mathematical techniques as essential tools in their work. 

In this panel discussion with women scientists across disciplines, physicist Sumati Surya will be in conversation with mathematician Rukmini Dey, physicist Dhavala Suri, cosmologist Pravabati Chingangbam and biochemist Shachi Gosavi. Together, they will discuss their research and the role mathematics plays in shaping it. 

This panel is part of the programming around the exhibition  'Women of Mathematics From Around the World'  on display at Science Gallery Bengaluru, from January 15 to March 22 2026.

name: Tell Me What You Eat: Food, Identity and Self
description:
How does the food we eat shape our identity and sense of self?

Join Dipali Khandelwal for a participatory conversation that reflects on the food that we grew up eating, the food we hide and the food that we have lost. Discover how food quietly shapes who we are, where we belong, and what we remember. Recognise disappearing food and the accompanying biodiversity loss. Understand how geographies and histories shape diets. Through this reflective session, learn to create food-identity records that can become part of a growing living archive in India.

name: A Bite of CALORIE: Malayalam Walkthrough
description: Join the Science Gallery Bengaluru team for a closer look at our new exhibition season, CALORIE, one bite at a time. This walkthrough, conducted in Malayalam, invites you to reflect on the ideas of food, nutrition, and energy. As we move through the exhibition, the team will unpack the intimate, and often complicated, relationships we share with food, nourishment, and the systems that sustain us.

name: In a State of Superposition
description:
Have you ever wondered what happens when ideas exist in superposition—when artists, technologists, and young thinkers collide like entangled particles to build quantum science together?

The Quantum Immersive Workshop, supported by the British Council, was a 14-day, hands-on programme that brought together young adults from varied academic and personal backgrounds to engage with quantum science through both artistic inquiry and technological experimentation. The participants explored how quantum ideas can be experienced, interpreted, and reimagined through practice-based work.

The outcomes of this workshop will be presented at the gallery, with the participants present to speak about their processes and insights. The showcase also offers an early glimpse into the conceptual and experiential direction of our upcoming QUANTUM exhibition-season.

name: Remembering the 1943 Bengal Famine: Hunger, Empathy and Violence
description:
How does a society remember a disaster like a famine?

In this talk Diya Gupta will look at archival letters, 1940s photographs, and artwork to examine how we might study the man-made 1943 Bengal Famine particularly through the eyes of the witnesses of the calamity. The talk will go beyond trying to establish what caused the famine and focus on the emotional responses of those who saw extreme hunger escalate before their eyes, and those who heard about it from afar. Going beyond Bengal, she will explore how food shortages affected large parts of South India too. And finally, with thoughts on how we might remember the famine today, she reflects on how that might recalibrate our own relationship with food and those we love.

name: Yele Oota
description:
When did nourishment become a commodity?

Explore this idea through the lens of the contemporary dance piece ‘Yele Oota’ performed by the Vishwakiran Nambi Dance Company. The performance explores humanity's most fundamental relationship, our connection with food and the act of sharing nourishment.

Through seven dancers and movement narratives, the choreography shifts between depicting resource accumulation and the gentle art of giving. Drawing from traditions where feeding others was considered the highest service, the piece celebrates the timeless wisdom of community kitchens and shared meals.

Using edible props that transform throughout the performance, ‘Yele Oota’ builds toward a powerful revelation: generosity is not merely a choice but a survival strategy for human civilisation.
